1: The LORD speaks to both Moses and Aaron together, as in Leviticus 14:33. The dual address signals that these discharge laws require both prophetic proclamation (Moses) and priestly administration (Aaron). Christ holds both offices: the prophet who declares the word and the priest who applies the atonement.
2: A man with a bodily discharge (zav — likely a chronic genital condition) is made unclean by his discharge. The uncleanness radiates from the body outward. This is the opposite direction from Christ's holiness, which radiates from his person outward to cleanse those he touches (Mark 1:41). The gospel inverts the contamination logic of the fall.
3: Whether the body lets the discharge flow or is blocked, the man is unclean. The uncleanness is tied to the condition itself, not to its visibility or activity at any given moment. Christ's cleansing addresses the underlying condition — not just its outward manifestations. He heals the source, not merely the symptoms.
4: Every bed the man with the discharge lies on becomes unclean. The contamination extends to his resting places — wherever he seeks rest becomes defiled. In Christ, the rest promised to the weary is clean and cleansing: Come to me all who are weary... and I will give you rest (Matt 11:28-29).
5: Touching the bed requires washing clothes and bathing — unclean until evening. The spreading uncleanness through touch establishes the logic that the hemorrhaging woman (Mark 5:25-34) navigated: she had to touch something, knowing her condition spread uncleanness to whatever she touched. Her desperation in reaching for Jesus was an act of faith precisely because she understood the system she was violating.
6: Sitting where the man sat also transmits uncleanness. Social proximity, shared space, ordinary human contact — all become vectors of impurity. The discharge laws give texture to the social exclusion Paul describes as the pre-conversion state: excluded from the commonwealth of Israel... having no hope and without God (Eph 2:12).
7: Touching the body of the man with the discharge spreads uncleanness. Direct contact is the primary transmission mechanism. Jesus accepted direct contact from the unclean — laying hands on lepers, touching the bier of the dead, being touched by the hemorrhaging woman — without the impurity vector working on him. His touch did not receive uncleanness; it transmitted cleanness.
8: If the man spits on a clean person, the clean person becomes unclean. Spitting is among the most intimate forms of bodily contact — associated with contempt and the transmission of what is internal to the external world. At his trial, Jesus was spat upon (Matt 26:67); he received the defilement of human contempt without being defiled by it.
9: Every saddle the man with the discharge rides on is unclean — his mode of transportation through the world becomes a source of spreading impurity. In Christ, the believer's journey through the world is covered: your life is hidden with Christ in God (Col 3:3).
10: Whatever was beneath him spreads uncleanness; carrying it requires washing. In Christ, he bore the weight of human uncleanness himself — taking on the contamination so that those who were beneath him might be raised with him (Col 2:12).
11: The man who touches the zav without having rinsed his hands becomes unclean. Even casual contact requires attention to preparedness. The Spirit's work in the believer cultivates exactly this kind of attentiveness to holiness and its conditions — a transformed conscience that monitors one's approach to the holy.
12: A clay vessel touched by the zav must be broken; a wooden vessel is merely rinsed. Clay cannot be purified by washing — it must be destroyed. Paul uses the clay vessel image for human bodies as containers of divine treasure (2 Cor 4:7). The old self — the clay pot shaped by the fall — must be broken, not merely rinsed; hence the dying and rising of baptism, not mere reformation.
13: When the discharge ceases, the man counts seven days, washes his clothes, bathes in running water, and is clean. The counting of seven days mirrors the creation week — a period of confirmed healing before restoration. Running water (mayim ḥayyim, living water) is the medium of cleansing — the same phrase Jesus uses for the Spirit (John 4:10-11; John 7:38).
14: On the eighth day, two turtledoves or two young pigeons are brought before the LORD at the tent of meeting. The same offering used for the poor leper's cleansing (Leviticus 14:22). The dove — Spirit symbol — completes both rites of restoration. Christ's baptism, marked by the Spirit's descent as a dove, inaugurates his ministry of restoring all who bear the marks of bodily and spiritual discharge.
15: The priest offers one bird as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering. Sin (the condition of uncleanness) and total dedication (the restored person's self-giving to God) are the two aspects of restoration. Christ both atones for sin and consecrates his people as a living sacrifice (Rom 12:1) in total dedication to God.
16: Seminal emission renders a man unclean until evening — a mild impurity resolved by nightfall. It acknowledges that even the most fundamental created gift of fertility participates in the impurity system. Christ's incarnation did not carry this impurity: born of a virgin (Luke 1:35), he entered the human condition without this transmission from Adam.
17: Any garment or leather with seminal emission must be washed — unclean until evening. The material world that contacts human physicality participates in its impurity. The comprehensive renewal in Christ extends to the physical: the resurrection of the body is the final answer to all such conditions, when the redeemed body no longer operates under the law of impurity.
18: Conjugal intimacy creates temporary impurity that resolves by evening. The fall has marked even the good gift of sexuality with the shadow of uncleanness. The marriage relationship is the arena Christ chooses to illustrate his union with the church (Eph 5:25-27), a union in which he presents the bride without spot or wrinkle... holy and without blemish.
19: A woman in her menstrual period (niddah) is unclean for seven days; whoever touches her is unclean until evening. The monthly cycle marks the female body with a recurring rhythm of impurity-and-restoration. Mary bore Jesus during the months when her own body was the site of God's incarnation — the holy and the ordinary creaturely rhythm intertwined in the one pregnancy that changes all of history.
20: Everything she lies on or sits on during the niddah period is unclean. As with the zav, the environment participates in the impurity. The thoroughgoing reach of the laws reflects how deeply uncleanness pervades ordinary structures of life. The coming of God's kingdom into these ordinary spaces is the good news: Christ comes into the niddah world, not away from it.
21: Touching her bed spreads uncleanness. The spreading-by-contact principle applies uniformly across all discharge categories. The consistency of the system teaches that no area of ordinary life is exempt from the human condition. The gospel's reach must be equally consistent: Christ's cleansing is not limited to spiritual matters but extends into the physical and relational dimensions these laws govern.
22: Touching anything she sat on spreads uncleanness. The surfaces of ordinary life — sitting places, resting places — are where the laws of uncleanness apply most concretely. Jesus' ministry was full of precisely these ordinary contacts: meals at people's homes, sitting in boats, touching ordinary people in ordinary places. His holiness engaged, rather than avoided, the ordinary contaminated world.
23: Whether on the bed or on anything she sat on — touching it brings uncleanness. The repetition reinforces comprehensiveness. No loophole exists. Similarly, the problem of human uncleanness before God admits no loophole: all have sinned (Rom 3:23); the only solution is atonement, not navigation around the system.
24: If a man sleeps with a woman during her menstrual period, he becomes unclean for seven days. The impurity of her state is transferred to the man who fully shares her condition through marital union. A full creation-week-length period of exclusion from worship — a sign of how far the created order has been affected by the fall, reaching even into its most intimate goods.
25: A woman with a chronic discharge of blood outside her normal cycle is in the niddah state for as long as the discharge continues. This is the exact condition of the woman in Mark 5:25-34 and Luke 8:43-48, who had suffered this for twelve years — perpetually unclean, perpetually excluded from normal communal and worship life.
26: Her bed during the discharge days is unclean as her menstrual bed; everything she sits on is unclean. For twelve years, every bed and every seat the hemorrhaging woman used was rendered unclean. The social isolation this implies is comprehensive: she could not sit in the synagogue, recline at meals, or rest in her household without spreading impurity. Her twelve-year exile was a living death.
27: Whoever touches these things becomes unclean. The woman of Mark 5 knew the system. She pressed through the crowd to touch Jesus — knowing she would spread uncleanness to everyone she brushed against, knowing she was violating the law's intent. Her desperation and faith drove her to the one place where she believed the impurity-logic might be reversed. And she was right: the power went from him to her (Mark 5:30).
28: When the discharge stops, she counts seven clean days and then is clean. After twelve years of perpetual discharge, the very moment of stopping would have inaugurated this seven-day count for the woman Jesus healed. Her immediate healing (Mark 5:29) launched her restoration; Jesus' declaration your faith has made you well (Mark 5:34) anticipates and ratifies the full restoration the law's process would complete.
29: On the eighth day she brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest. The same eighth-day offering as the cleansed male zav (Leviticus 15:14). Male and female receive identical restoration procedure. There is no gendered hierarchy in the means of atonement — both are restored through the same priestly intercession and the same dove sacrifice.
30: One dove as sin offering, one as burnt offering; the priest makes atonement before the LORD. The complete restoration formula: sin addressed (ḥatta't), total dedication given (olah), atonement made. The hemorrhaging woman's restoration in Christ is this complete: not partial healing but full atonement and reconstitution as a daughter of Abraham (Luke 13:16).
31: You shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst. The purpose clause of all purity law: YHWH's tabernacle presence is simultaneously the greatest gift and the gravest danger. In Christ, this danger is permanently resolved: God no longer merely tabernacles among us but in us (1 Cor 6:19), having absorbed the deadly collision of holiness and uncleanness in the cross.
32: This is the law for the man with a discharge, the man who has a seminal emission and becomes unclean by it. The masculine summary closes the male discharge laws (Leviticus 15:2-18). Christ, assuming full human embodiment, entered this full range of creaturely experience without incurring any of its impurity — and then took the penalty for all of it (2 Cor 5:21).
33: Summary of all discharge categories: the woman in menstrual separation, the man or woman with any discharge, the man who lies with an unclean woman. The closing summary universalizes: all human beings across all categories of bodily discharge need priestly atonement for restoration to God's presence. The comprehensive need finds comprehensive answer in Christ, the one sacrifice that covers all categories and all persons for all time (Heb 10:12).
